questions.question: What is cryptographic agility?

options.text: Using the fastest available algorithm
The ability to switch algorithms without major system changes
Automatically upgrading to new algorithms
Using multiple algorithms simultaneously

questions.explanation: Cryptographic agility means designing systems so that algorithms can be swapped out with minimal code changes. This is essential for responding to security advisories or transitioning to new standards like PQC.

questions.question: When planning security margins, what should you consider for 30-year data protection?

options.text: Use the minimum acceptable security level today
Use the highest available security level plus expect some erosion
Security margins are not relevant for long-term planning
Only quantum-safe algorithms matter, security bits are irrelevant

questions.explanation: Security margins erode over time due to cryptanalysis advances and hardware improvements. For 30-year protection, start with the highest security level and account for expected erosion (roughly 2 bits per decade).

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for the hybrid deployment phase?

options.text: Use only classical algorithms
Use only PQC algorithms
Combine classical and PQC algorithms together
Randomly alternate between classical and PQC

questions.explanation: During hybrid deployment, systems use both classical and PQC algorithms together. This provides security against both classical attacks (proven classical algorithms) and quantum attacks (PQC), with defense in depth during the transition.

questions.question: Which systems should be migrated to PQC first?

options.text: Systems with the easiest migration path
Systems processing critical/high-sensitivity data
The oldest systems in the organization
Systems with the most users

questions.explanation: Systems handling critical or high-sensitivity data should be prioritized for PQC migration. These systems face the highest risk from HNDL attacks and need quantum protection earliest.

questions.question: What is a downgrade attack in algorithm negotiation?

options.text: Using an older version of the software
Forcing negotiation to select a weaker algorithm than available
Reducing the key size during transmission
Slowing down the encryption process

questions.explanation: A downgrade attack manipulates the negotiation process to force selection of a weaker algorithm even when both parties support stronger options. This can be prevented by signing algorithm offers and rejecting unexpected weak algorithms.

questions.question: What does algorithm lifecycle status 'deprecated' mean?

options.text: The algorithm is completely broken
The algorithm should be phased out due to security concerns
The algorithm is recommended for new systems
The algorithm is still in research phase

questions.explanation: Deprecated status means the algorithm has known weaknesses or concerns and should be phased out of use. It may still work but is no longer recommended for new deployments, and existing uses should plan migration.

questions.question: Once migrated to PQC, organizations no longer need to plan for future algorithm changes.
questions.explanation: Cryptographic algorithms have finite lifetimes. Even after PQC migration, organizations should maintain crypto agility to handle future discoveries, new standards, or improved algorithms. The NIST process itself is ongoing with more algorithms being evaluated.
